clusters.title: Troy Jackson set to become Democratic Maine Senate nominee, replacing Platner
clusters.neutral_headline: Troy Jackson set to become Democratic Maine Senate nominee, replacing Platner
bullet_summary: Reported by CBS News, Politico

items.title: Troy Jackson set to become Democratic Maine Senate nominee, replacing Platner
items.standfirst: Former state senator Troy Jackson, who calls himself a "pickup progressive," is set to become Maine's Democratic Senate nominee. He'll replace Graham Platner, who left the race following accusations of sexual assault. Fin Gómez reports.
items.content: Former state senator Troy Jackson, who calls himself a "pickup progressive," is set to become Maine's Democratic Senate nominee. He'll replace Graham Platner, who left the race following accusations of sexual assault. Fin Gómez reports.

items.title: Troy Jackson has effectively secured the Maine Senate Democratic nomination
items.standfirst: Jackson’s preferred delegates have won a majority of the seats at next week’s nominating convention to replace Graham Platner.
items.content: SANFORD, Maine — Troy Jackson has effectively secured the Democratic nomination in the crucial Maine Senate race, winning the support of a majority of the state delegates who will officially nominate a candidate next weekend. While they are not legally bound to support him, more than two-thirds of the 601 delegates have committed to voting for Jackson at that statewide convention. Jackson dominated the county-level meetings this weekend that selected those delegates, with his rivals falling far behind — and his two most organized competitors, Maine Secretary of State Shenna Bellows and former state public health official Nirav Shah, dropped out of the race on Sunday. The party’s statewide convention in Bangor, Maine, next weekend will cap a tumultuous scramble to replace Graham Platner, who won the primary but dropped out this month after POLITICO reported that a woman he previously dated said he forced her to have sex with him. Platner denied the allegations. A progressive 5th generation logger from the far-northern corner of Allagash, Maine, Jackson has had a long career in organizing progressive activists and ties to Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), who had endorsed him in his gubernatorial race earlier this year. The candidate will have less than four months to spin up a major battleground Senate campaign against GOP Sen. Susan Collins, the only Senate Republican running for reelection in a state carried by former Vice President Kamala Harris in 2024.Jackson turned his attention to the general election at a Sunday morning rally ahead of the delegate meeting in York County, where he secured the support of enough delegates to cross the 50 percent threshold. “Whether you supported me from the beginning, or are still deciding, or support another candidate, there is a place for you in this movement,” he told a few dozen supporters in a school parking lot in Sanford, Maine, echoing what has been a familiar line in his speeches since entering the Senate race. “Because we're not going to defeat Susan Collins by making this movement smaller. There's no way that that's ever going to work.” Bellows dropped out of the race just minutes before voting began on Sunday and encouraged Democrats to coalesce behind Jackson. “This has been an unprecedented nominating process, compressed into days instead of months, and I'm grateful to every volunteer who worked around the clock for this movement,” she said in a statement. “That energy is exactly what we need to beat Susan Collins in November — and Democrats don't have a day to waste in unifying around that shared goal.” Jackson learned of his victory while speaking with Democrats at the Sagadahoc County meeting at a high school in Topsham, Maine, when an aide whispered in his ear the results of York County’s caucus. Jackson reacted with a small head nod before continuing his conversations with voters. News of his win ignited audible excitement from Jackson volunteers at the meeting. One organizer sprinted across the school’s gym to show Jackson the news on their phone. “It’s incredible,” Jackson told POLITICO moments after he secured a majority of delegates. “I’m excited, but very tempered, very measured about my emotions other than the fact that a lot of really great people made this happen.”Jackson’s whirlwind path to the nomination is the prologue to an even tougher test. Collins has defeated well-funded Democrats of all stripes since she was first elected in 1996, and currently has an $11 million war chest in reserves, according to the latest filings from the Federal Election Commission. And Jackson’s last-minute ascension puts him at an even greater disadvantage: Maine’s nominating convention on Saturday is just 101 days before Election Day in November, giving Jackson less time to run against Collins than Harris had in her losing campaign against Donald Trump in 2024. “We’re going to have to put our ground game even more into maximum overdrive,” Jackson told reporters after his Sanford rally, referencing Platner’s large number of town halls and his short timeline. “What did Graham have, 70 town halls? If we did one every other day we wouldn’t be able to do 70 from here on out.” Jackson also said he will have to quickly introduce himself to the southern part of the state, given he hails from the far-north and represented the area in the state Senate. “I’m not as well known down here,” he said. “I’m six hours away from my home. And as we know, the state legislature means nothing. Nobody’s following it. It’s a lot of work to make sure people know what I’m about.” Many former Platner supporters were part of the team of volunteers, organizers and delegate nominees that whipped votes for Jackson’s delegate slate in counties across Maine over the weekend. The strength of his organizing operation helped Jackson jump out to a commanding lead after Saturday’s meetings, including a sweep of all 149 delegates in Cumberland County, the largest county in the state. Travis Jones, a former Platner supporter from Seal Harbor, Maine, who was elected as a delegate alternate in Hancock County, where Platner lives, said he’s backing Jackson because of the similarities between Platner’s progressive agenda and the Jackson campaign. “I was a big supporter of the previous candidate, Graham Platner. I really liked his policies. Troy mirrors a lot of those,” Jones said ahead of the delegate meeting in Ellsworth, Maine, on Saturday. “The previous campaign really energized me, and here I am today.”

items.title: Maine Democrat Shenna Bellows drops out of race to replace Graham Platner
items.standfirst: She ended her Senate bid on Sunday as rival Troy Jackson consolidated support in the state’s delegation selection process.
items.content: SANFORD, Maine — Maine Secretary of State Shenna Bellows ended her Senate campaign on Sunday, further clearing the path for rival Troy Jackson as he consolidates support in a fast-moving Democratic nomination contest. "This has been an unprecedented nominating process, compressed into days instead of months, and I'm grateful to every volunteer who worked around the clock for this movement," Bellows said in a statement. "That energy is exactly what we need to beat Susan Collins in November — and Democrats don't have a day to waste in unifying around that shared goal." Bellows was the most prominent woman to enter the breakneck contest, which has unfolded over the last week after Graham Platner ended his campaign following POLITICO’s report that a woman who used to date him accused him of sexual assault. He has denied the allegations. Maine Democrats stood up a rapid nominating process, with county parties selecting delegates to go to a convention next weekend. It became quickly clear that Jackson, the former Maine Senate president who is aligned with organized labor and national progressives, was the frontrunner. On the first day of the local selection process, Jackson's volunteer operation dominated and won most of the slates. The stakes in the nomination fight are high for Democrats: Collins is among the most vulnerable Republican senators up for reelection this November. But she has been a resilient campaigner for decades in blue-leaning Maine, and the Platner fiasco may cast a shadow over the general election. Bellows was one of several Democrats, including Jackson, who ran for governor this year but lost the primary — and then jumped into the Senate contest after Platner dropped out. Her halting delivery during the Senate primary debate earlier this week and her struggle to stand out among the crowded field eventually led her to end her campaign.

clusters.title: Lindsey Graham's sister, Darline, announces bid for full Senate term in South Carolina
clusters.neutral_headline: Nancy Mace won’t run for Lindsey Graham’s Senate seat
bullet_summary: Lindsey Graham's term in the Senate, said she will seek a full term for the South Carolina seat her brother held for more than two decades
Reported by CBS News, Politico

items.title: Lindsey Graham's sister, Darline, announces bid for full Senate term in South Carolina
items.standfirst: Darline Graham, the sister of late Sen. Lindsey Graham, says she will run for a full Senate term in South Carolina. CBS News political director Fin Gómez has more.
items.content: Darline Graham, the sister of late Sen. Lindsey Graham, says she will run for a full Senate term in South Carolina. CBS News political director Fin Gómez has more.

items.title: Nancy Mace won’t run for Lindsey Graham’s Senate seat
items.standfirst: Her decision comes after an unsuccessful run for governor earlier this year, and after President Donald Trump backed Sen. Darline Graham for a full term.
items.content: Firebrand Republican Rep. Nancy Mace announced Monday that she will not run for the South Carolina Senate seat held by the late Lindsey Graham. “I'm not running for the U.S. Senate, not because I'm backing down from a fight, but because the one I'm already in matters right now,” she said in a video announcement. Her decision comes after a fifth-place primary finish in her run for governor earlier this year and as the race to succeed Graham is already taking shape. Mace began exploring the viability of a potential bid just hours after Graham’s death became public, two people familiar with her thinking told POLITICO at the time, but decided against a second statewide bid in as many months. Her move effectively guarantees she will not be serving in elected office once her term ends early next year. In the video, Mace said “the laws meant to protect me and other women failed us, and I won't stop until that changes.” “Serving South Carolina has been the honor of my life, and I will continue to voice our values everywhere I can; I’m even going to shit post from time to time, because someone has to call out the dumbasses,” she said. Sen. Darline Graham, Lindsey Graham’s sister who was appointed to serve out the remainder of his term shortly after his death, announced Monday that she is running for a full term, and she has already secured President Donald Trump’s endorsement. GOP Reps. Russell Fry and Ralph Norman have announced bids as well. The filing window for the special primary election opens Tuesday and closes one week later. The election will be held Aug. 11, with a runoff for the top two candidates if no one wins 50 percent of the vote Aug. 25. Whoever wins the special GOP primary will be the heavy favorite to secure a full term in November.

items.title: Darline Graham, Lindsey Graham's sister, says she'll run for full Senate term
items.standfirst: Sen. Darline Graham, who is serving out the remainder of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham's term in the Senate, said she will seek a full term for the South Carolina seat her brother held for more than two decades.
items.content: Sen. Darline Graham, who is serving out the remainder of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham's term in the Senate, said she will seek a full term for the South Carolina seat her brother held for more than two decades.

clusters.title: At least 930 people have died in Congo's Ebola outbreak
clusters.neutral_headline: At least 930 people have died in Congo's Ebola outbreak
bullet_summary: Congo's health ministry said the deaths were recorded out of the 2,344 cases confirmed as of Saturday
The Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) has now claimed 930 lives, the country's health ministry has said

items.title: At least 930 people have died in Congo's Ebola outbreak
items.standfirst: Congo's health ministry said the deaths were recorded out of the 2,344 cases confirmed as of Saturday. Thirty-seven new deaths were recorded in the previous 24 hours in one of the highest totals.
items.content: Congo's health ministry said the deaths were recorded out of the 2,344 cases confirmed as of Saturday. Thirty-seven new deaths were recorded in the previous 24 hours in one of the highest totals.(Image credit: Moses Sawasawa)
